required.title: [Tools] Pliers (standard and needle-nose), adjustable wrench, set of wrenches or socket set, wire cutters
[Materials] Steel wire (various gauges from 20-gauge to 12-gauge), thin sheet metal (26-gauge or 28-gauge aluminum or steel), assorted bolts, nuts, and washers

suggested.title: [Activity] Visit a blacksmith shop, a fabrication shop, or a machine shop

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Safety data sheets for metals being handled

competence_markers.description: The student can identify steel, aluminum, copper, brass, and stainless steel by sight, weight, magnetism, and feel
The student can correctly assemble and tighten bolt-nut-washer fastener assemblies with the correct sizes and appropriate tightness
The student can bend wire to planned shapes, demonstrating understanding of springback
The student can form simple shapes from thin sheet metal and file cut edges smooth
The student handles metal safely: aware of sharp edges, heat conduction, filings, and weight
The student cleans workspace thoroughly after every session, including sweeping metal filings and securing sharp scraps

content.why_this_matters:
Look around you. Metal is everywhere. The hinges on your door. The nails in your walls. The frame of your bicycle. The engine in your car. The tools in your workshop. Metal holds the modern world together — literally. Without metal, there are no bridges, no buildings over a few stories, no vehicles, no machines, no electricity flowing through wires to your home.

You are going to learn about metal the way you learn about any material — by handling it, working with it, and understanding what it can do and what it demands. Metal is different from wood. It is harder. It is heavier. It conducts heat — which means it gets hot when you work it and cold when you leave it outside in winter. It has sharp edges that can cut you without warning. And it is incredibly strong, which is why we build the most important structures in the world with it.

At this level, you will not be welding or using power tools on metal. You will be doing something more important: developing respect for the material. You will learn to identify different metals by sight, weight, and feel. You will learn that metals have different properties — some are hard, some are soft, some bend easily, some resist bending. You will work with metal fasteners, bend wire and sheet metal with your hands, and build simple projects that teach you how metal behaves.

This is the foundation. Everything that follows in metalworking — cutting, welding, fabrication — depends on understanding what metal is and respecting what it can do.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Metal identification** — the student can distinguish common metals by sight, weight, magnetism, and feel
2. **Understanding metal properties** — hardness, weight, magnetism, flexibility, and conductivity as observable characteristics
3. **Fastener literacy** — understanding bolts, nuts, washers, and how metal pieces are joined mechanically
4. **Cold forming basics** — bending wire and thin sheet metal to understand how metal resists and yields to force
5. **Safety awareness** — understanding that metal edges cut, metal surfaces conduct heat, and metal filings are sharp
6. **Respect for the material** — developing the serious attention that metal demands
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Metal Is

Metal is a material that comes from the earth. Most metals are found in rocks called ores — rock that contains metal mixed with other minerals. The metal is separated from the rock by heating it to very high temperatures in a process called smelting. Humans have been doing this for thousands of years. The Bronze Age — one of the great periods of human history — is named after the metal alloy (bronze) that changed civilization. The Iron Age followed, and iron and steel are still the backbone of everything we build.

What makes metal special? Several things:

**Strength.** Metal can carry enormous loads without breaking. A steel beam the size of your forearm can support the weight of a car. This is why the frames of buildings, bridges, and machines are made of metal.

**Hardness.** Metal resists being scratched, dented, or worn away. This is why we make cutting tools from metal — a steel saw blade cuts through wood because the steel is harder than the wood.

**Malleability.** Metal can be shaped. It can be bent, hammered, pressed, and rolled into almost any form without breaking (up to a point — bend it too far or too many times and it will crack). This is why metal can be formed into complex shapes: car bodies, pipes, wire, tools.

**Conductivity.** Metal conducts heat and electricity. This is why cooking pots are metal (heat flows from the burner through the metal to your food) and why electrical wires are metal (electricity flows through metal easily).

**Durability.** Metal lasts. A well-maintained metal structure can stand for centuries. The Eiffel Tower is made of iron and has stood since 1889. Steel bridges built a hundred years ago still carry traffic today.

### Part 2: Identifying Common Metals

You will learn to identify five common metals by their observable properties. This is not a memorization exercise — it is training your senses to read material the way a craftsman does.

**Steel (mild/carbon steel):**
- Color: Dark gray, sometimes with a blue-black scale from manufacturing
- Weight: Heavy — noticeably heavier than aluminum of the same size
- Magnetic: Yes — a magnet sticks firmly
- Feel: Hard, rigid, does not bend easily in your hands (in thick sections)
- Where you see it: Nails, bolts, structural beams, car frames, tools, rebar

**Aluminum:**
- Color: Light silver-gray, often with a slightly dull surface
- Weight: Light — noticeably lighter than steel of the same size (about one-third the weight)
- Magnetic: No — a magnet slides right off
- Feel: Softer than steel, bends more easily
- Where you see it: Soda cans, aircraft, window frames, cooking foil, some ladders

**Copper:**
- Color: Distinctive reddish-orange (or green when weathered — the Statue of Liberty is copper)
- Weight: Heavy — heavier than steel for the same size
- Magnetic: No
- Feel: Soft, bends easily, smooth
- Where you see it: Electrical wire (inside the plastic coating), plumbing pipes, pennies (before 1982)

**Brass:**
- Color: Gold-yellow
- Weight: Heavy
- Magnetic: No
- Feel: Smooth, slightly softer than steel
- Where you see it: Keys, door hinges, musical instruments (trumpets, tubas), decorative hardware

**Stainless steel:**
- Color: Bright silver, shiny
- Weight: Similar to mild steel — heavy
- Magnetic: Sometimes (depends on the type — some stainless is magnetic, some is not)
- Feel: Hard, smooth, cool to the touch
- Where you see it: Kitchen sinks, silverware, appliances, surgical tools

**The identification test:** Pick up a piece of metal. What color is it? How heavy is it compared to its size? Does a magnet stick to it? How hard is it — can you scratch it with another piece of metal? Can you bend it? These questions, asked in order, will identify most common metals correctly.

### Part 3: Fasteners — How Metal Pieces Join Together

Metal pieces are joined together in three main ways: mechanical fasteners (bolts, screws, rivets), welding (melting the pieces together), and adhesives (specialized glues). At this tier, you will work with mechanical fasteners.

**The bolt-nut-washer assembly:** This is the most common mechanical fastener in the world. A bolt passes through holes in the pieces being joined. A nut threads onto the bolt from the other side. Tightening the nut pulls the pieces together.

- **The bolt** has a head (which the wrench grips) and a threaded shaft. The threads are a spiral ridge that the nut grips as it turns.
- **The nut** has internal threads that match the bolt's external threads. Turning the nut clockwise tightens it (righty-tighty). Turning it counterclockwise loosens it (lefty-loosey).
- **The washer** sits between the nut and the surface of the material. It distributes the clamping force over a wider area so the nut does not dig into the material or pull through.

**Proper tightening:** A bolt should be tight enough that the pieces cannot move, but not so tight that the bolt stretches or the threads strip. Over-tightening is one of the most common mistakes beginners make — more is not always better with fasteners. You should feel firm resistance and then stop. If you keep turning past firm, you are damaging the fastener.

**Size matters:** Bolts and nuts come in specific sizes, and they must match. A 3/8-inch bolt takes a 3/8-inch nut. Trying to force the wrong size nut onto a bolt damages both. The wrench must also match — a wrench that is too large for the bolt head will round off the corners, making the bolt nearly impossible to remove later.

### Part 4: Cold Forming — What Happens When You Bend Metal

Pick up a piece of wire and bend it. You are doing something called cold forming — changing the shape of metal without heating it.

Notice what happens:

- The wire resists bending at first. Metal does not want to change shape. This resistance is called stiffness.
- Once you bend it past a certain point, it stays bent. It does not spring all the way back. This is called plastic deformation — the metal has permanently changed shape.
- But it springs back a little. This is called springback. If you want a 90-degree bend, you have to bend slightly past 90 degrees because the metal will spring back partially.
- If you bend the wire back and forth in the same spot several times, it gets hot at the bend point (feel it — carefully) and eventually breaks. This is called fatigue. The metal's structure breaks down from repeated bending.

These are not just facts about wire. They are fundamental properties of all metals. The same springback that affects your wire affects a factory pressing car body panels. The same fatigue that breaks your wire eventually breaks airplane parts if they are not inspected and replaced. What you are learning with a piece of wire applies to every metal structure in the world.

**Sheet metal forming:** Take a piece of thin sheet metal (26-gauge or 28-gauge). Bend it over the edge of a table or a piece of wood. Notice:
- The bend is sharp where the metal contacts the edge
- The sheet metal wants to spring back, just like the wire
- You can create a box shape by making multiple bends
- Sharp edges form where you bend — these edges can cut you. Always handle bent sheet metal carefully and file or fold sharp edges.

### Part 5: Safety with Metal

Metal demands respect in specific ways that differ from wood:

**Sharp edges.** Cut metal has edges that are razor-sharp. Sheet metal edges, cut ends of wire, burrs left from drilling or cutting — all of these will cut you cleanly and deeply before you feel it. Always assume that any cut edge of metal is sharp. Handle cut metal edges with work gloves or carefully, never by running your hand along the edge.

**Heat conduction.** Metal conducts heat rapidly. A metal piece that is being cut, drilled, ground, or welded gets hot — and the heat travels through the entire piece. You can be burned by touching a part of a metal piece that is far from where the work is happening. Before touching any metal that has been worked on, test it carefully with the back of your hand held near the surface (do not grab it).

**Filings and chips.** When metal is cut, drilled, filed, or ground, it produces tiny pieces of metal — filings, chips, and shavings. These pieces are sharp. They can embed in your skin, damage your eyes, and cause puncture wounds in your feet if you are not wearing proper shoes. Safety glasses are required whenever metal is being cut or filed. Sweeping up metal filings is part of cleanup, every time.

**Weight.** Metal is heavy. A short piece of steel bar stock weighs more than you might expect. Always lift metal with your legs, not your back. Never carry more than you can hold securely. Set metal down carefully — dropping it on your foot can break bones, and dropping it on a hard floor creates a tripping hazard.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Metal Identification
**Developing well:** The student reliably identifies steel, aluminum, copper, brass, and stainless steel using observable properties. They can explain their reasoning: "It is light and not magnetic, so it is aluminum." They handle metals with confidence and curiosity.

**Developing slowly:** The student confuses metals, relies on a single property (only color, only magnetism) without cross-checking, or cannot identify metals that look different from their initial samples (e.g., rusty steel versus clean steel).

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student handled enough variety of samples? Aluminum from a soda can looks different from aluminum angle stock. Rusty steel looks different from galvanized steel. Provide diverse samples and discuss why the same metal can look different in different forms.

### 2. Fastener Skills
**Developing well:** The student matches bolts to correct nut sizes, assembles bolt-washer-nut correctly, selects the right wrench, and tightens to firm without over-tightening. The process is smooth and confident.

**Developing slowly:** The student grabs random nuts for bolts, forces mismatched sizes, over-tightens everything, or cannot select the correct wrench size.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the student have enough practice with a variety of fastener sizes? Have you demonstrated what over-tightening does (stripped threads, rounded heads)? Have you shown them a bolt that was damaged by the wrong wrench size?

### 3. Safety Awareness
**Developing well:** The student automatically checks for sharp edges before handling cut metal, wears gloves when appropriate, files edges after cutting, sweeps up filings, and handles heavy pieces carefully. This behavior is consistent without reminders.

**Developing slowly:** The student grabs metal without checking edges, forgets filings cleanup, or handles heavy pieces carelessly.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you been consistent in modeling safety behavior yourself? Have you explained the specific consequences (cuts from edges, eye injury from filings, foot injury from dropped metal) in concrete terms the student understands?
